skillName: Angles on a straight line sum to 180°
misconceptionSummary: Students forget that angles on a straight line must add up to 180° and instead try to guess missing angles without using the rule.

visuals.ruleText: Angles on a straight line add up to 180°
visuals.subText: Set up an equation: known angle + unknown angle = 180°, then solve

steps.narration: Any angles that sit on one side of a straight line and share the same point must add to one hundred and eighty degrees. To find a missing angle, write the known angles plus the unknown equal to one hundred and eighty, then solve.

lines.text: Two angles on a straight line: 65° and x°

steps.narration: Two angles sit on a straight line: sixty-five degrees and the unknown x. Write the equation: sixty-five plus x equals one hundred and eighty. Subtract sixty-five: x equals one hundred and fifteen degrees.

lines.text: Three angles on a line: 40°, 75°, and y°

steps.narration: Three angles: forty, seventy-five, and the unknown y. Sum to one hundred and eighty: forty plus seventy-five is one hundred and fifteen, so y equals one hundred and eighty minus one hundred and fifteen, which is sixty-five degrees.

visuals.label: Sum all angles on the line = 180° — subtract known angles to find the unknown

steps.narration: One hundred and fifteen and sixty-five. Both check sum to one hundred and eighty. The rule is the same however many angles are on the line — sum them all to one hundred and eighty.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: guessing or using 360° for angles on a straight line. The rule is 180° — a straight line is a half turn. Set up the equation: known angles + unknown = 180°.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Angles on a straight line always sum to 180°. Write an equation, substitute the known angles, and subtract to find the unknown. Never guess — the rule gives an exact answer.
